title: AC Not Cooling in Tampa, FL — Field Diagnostic & Decision Guide

summary_30s.headline: When the outdoor unit runs but comfort falls behind in Tampa humidity, the fastest split is airflow versus refrigerant performance — guessing wastes money.

top_causes.label: Compressor or metering device underperforming
top_causes.probability: Lower until charge and airflow are proven

summary_30s.core_truth: No amount of thermostat lowering fixes a system that cannot move enough air or cannot absorb/reject heat at design rates.
summary_30s.risk_warning: Running a low-charge system hard can cook the compressor; full changeouts in Tampa often land $6,000–$12,000 when a $1,500–$3,500 leak repair plus proper charge would have saved the unit.

quick_checks.check: Feel supply vs return: strong temperature split?
quick_checks.homeowner: Hold a thermometer strip near a supply register with the fan on.
quick_checks.result_meaning: Weak split with warm supply usually means airflow or charge, not thermostat.
quick_checks.next_step: If split is poor, change filter and verify all registers open before calling.
quick_checks.risk: Ignoring a frozen coil can flood the drain pan and damage ceilings.

quick_checks.check: Inspect indoor coil through access for icing or dirt matting
quick_checks.homeowner: Look for frost lines or black matting on coil face.
quick_checks.result_meaning: Icing with low airflow mimics low charge; both raise head pressure.
quick_checks.next_step: Turn cooling off, fan on, and stop DIY if ice is heavy.
quick_checks.risk: Chipping ice off fins destroys the coil — replacement commonly $2,500–$6,000.

quick_checks.check: Outdoor condenser: clean fins and verify fan spins at full speed
quick_checks.homeowner: Power off at disconnect, rinse fins gently from inside out.
quick_checks.result_meaning: Dead head pressure from blocked condenser reads like refrigerant fault.
quick_checks.next_step: If fan is slow or loud after cleaning, book a pro same-week.
quick_checks.risk: Overheated compressor valves fail; compressor-only jobs often exceed $2,000 parts plus labor.

diagnostic_steps.step: Confirm mechanical cooling is actually being requested
diagnostic_steps.homeowner: Mode cool, setpoint several degrees below room, fan auto or on.
diagnostic_steps.pro: Verify Y call, contactor pull-in, and liquid/suction line behavior under load.
diagnostic_steps.risk: Chasing refrigerant on a stat mis-wire burns time and refrigerant.

diagnostic_steps.step: Measure static pressure and airflow sanity before gauges
diagnostic_steps.homeowner: Note weak rooms, crushed flex, or closed dampers.
diagnostic_steps.pro: Static test and blower amps tell if duct or blower is the ceiling on performance.
diagnostic_steps.risk: Adding charge on restricted airflow drives superheat chaos and slugging risk.

diagnostic_steps.step: Refrigerant circuit only after airflow is ruled in
diagnostic_steps.homeowner: Do not buy DIY cans — illegal mishandling and wrong charge.
diagnostic_steps.pro: Weigh-in, leak search with bubble/micro leak protocol, subcooling/superheat targets.
diagnostic_steps.risk: Overcharge destroys compressor; neglected leaks can mean $1,800–$4,500 compressor changeout.

nodes.label: AC not cooling — Tampa heat load
Airflow path: filter, coil, blower, ducts
Condenser rejection: clean, fan, charge signs

repair_matrix.issue: Clogged filter / minor coil surface dirt
Refrigerant leak on indoor coil or flare

cost_escalation.description: Running longer cycles, higher humidity indoors.
Compressor overheats daily; coil freezes intermittently.
Compressor damage or coil replacement plus full charge.

safe: Swap pleated filter if dirty and rerun with dry coil.
Rinse condenser fins after disconnect verified off.
call_pro: No temperature split after filter and clean attempts.
Hissing, oil stains, or icing returns within hours.
stop_now: Burning electrical smell from air handler or outdoor unit.
Breaker trips immediately when cooling engages.

final_warning: Do not keep resetting breakers or forcing a frozen system to run; you risk fire, water damage, and compressor destruction.
cta: If two quick checks fail or any STOP sign appears, shut the system off and schedule a licensed Tampa HVAC tech for same-week leak and airflow verification.

title: AC Not Turning On in Tampa, FL — Power, Control, and Start Components

summary_30s.headline: A dead air conditioner is usually power delivery, thermostat logic, or a failed start component — sequence matters so you do not replace the wrong part.

top_causes.label: Tripped breaker, blown fuse, or float switch lockout
top_causes.probability: Very common after storms or drain clogs

top_causes.label: Thermostat batteries, scheduling, or wiring fault
top_causes.probability: Common on retrofits and smart stat swaps

summary_30s.core_truth: If the indoor fan runs on cool but the outdoor unit never pulls in, the problem is almost always the high-voltage path or the outdoor control stack.
summary_30s.risk_warning: Repeatedly resetting a shorted circuit can arc damage the panel; neglected compressor lockout can escalate to a $1,600–$5,500 outdoor section replacement versus a $180–$450 start kit repair if caught early.

quick_checks.check: Main panel and HVAC disconnect: any tripped breaker or pulled disconnect?
quick_checks.homeowner: Flip fully off then on; verify outdoor disconnect seated.
quick_checks.result_meaning: If breaker trips instantly, you have a hard fault.
quick_checks.next_step: Leave off and call if instant trip repeats.
quick_checks.risk: Forcing a shorted compressor to energize can weld contacts.

quick_checks.check: Condensate pan float switch: lifted arm or wet switch?
quick_checks.homeowner: Look for water in secondary pan or blinking stat message.
quick_checks.result_meaning: Many installs cut Y when float opens — looks like dead AC.
quick_checks.next_step: Clear primary drain if safe; otherwise stop and call.
quick_checks.risk: Bypassing a float masks active flooding and rots cabinets.

quick_checks.check: Outdoor contactor pulled in? Humming with no fan spin?
quick_checks.homeowner: Listen across the fence line; do not touch live parts.
quick_checks.result_meaning: Pull-in without fan often capacitor; no pull-in often low voltage or bad stat.
quick_checks.next_step: Photo model plate for tech; do not poke terminals.
quick_checks.risk: 240V exposure kills — stay outside the electrical compartment.

diagnostic_steps.step: Verify 24VAC control at stat and at outdoor board
diagnostic_steps.homeowner: Confirm mode cool and fan behavior per manual.
diagnostic_steps.pro: Measure R–C, Y call presence, fuse on board, transformer load.

diagnostic_steps.step: Inspect contactor pits and ants across coil
diagnostic_steps.homeowner: Visual only through grille if cover allows.
diagnostic_steps.pro: Replace pitted contactor; verify coil resistance.
diagnostic_steps.risk: Chattering contactor welds shut and runs unit unsupervised.

diagnostic_steps.step: Capacitor and hard start kit evaluation
diagnostic_steps.homeowner: Never discharge caps DIY without training.
diagnostic_steps.pro: ESR test, microfarad within tolerance, start assist sizing.
diagnostic_steps.risk: Wrong dual cap size cooks the new cap and motor windings.

nodes.label: Power path: panel, disconnect, door switch
Control path: stat, float, low voltage fuse
Outdoor start: contactor, cap, short tests

repair_matrix.fix: Clear trap, blow line, add safety pan treatment

safe: Reset float after verified dry pan and cleared visible clog.
Replace thermostat batteries and re-seat stat on base.
call_pro: Breaker trips twice in a row when AC tries to start.
Outdoor hums, clicks, but never spins fan or compressor.
stop_now: Smoke, scorch marks, or melted wire insulation at disconnect.
Oily residue at compressor terminals with acrid smell.

final_warning: High voltage kills trained techs who get careless — never open energized outdoor compartments as a homeowner.
cta: If power checks out but the outdoor never starts, shut the disconnect and book a Tampa tech for low-voltage tracing and safe capacitor testing.

title: Weak Airflow in Tampa, FL — Duct, Filter, and Blower Diagnostics

summary_30s.headline: Weak airflow is a capacity killer in Florida humidity: the coil cannot exchange latent load, so rooms feel clammy even if refrigerant charge is technically fine.

top_causes.label: Oversized pleated filter or collapsed return duct
Dirty blower wheel or clogged evaporator
top_causes.probability: High in pet and pollen homes

summary_30s.core_truth: CFM is the ceiling on BTU delivery; starving the blower raises coil temperature and humidity complaints faster than a one-degree setpoint change.
summary_30s.risk_warning: Low airflow over seasons overheats heat strips in winter package units and strains blower motors; full duct redesigns can exceed $1,500–$6,000 when ignored until multiple rooms fail.

quick_checks.check: Return grille pull: does filter pull out hard against suction?
quick_checks.homeowner: With fan on, feel if a cheap filter caves inward.
quick_checks.result_meaning: High negative static often means filter too dense or return too small.
quick_checks.next_step: Temporarily test with MERV 8 panel if manufacturer allows.
quick_checks.risk: Running MERV 13 on marginal ductwork silently kills compressors.

quick_checks.check: Room-to-room balance: one weak branch only?
quick_checks.homeowner: Open dampers fully; listen for whistling in one bay.
quick_checks.result_meaning: Localized restriction points to crushed flex or stuck damper.
quick_checks.next_step: Photo attic flex runs for the tech before moving insulation.
quick_checks.risk: Walking attic joists unprotected risks falls — not worth a filter swap.

quick_checks.check: Blower door panel sealed and correct speed tap?
quick_checks.homeowner: Verify panel clips latched; note rattling wheel noise.
quick_checks.result_meaning: Unsealed panels bypass return and skew airflow readings.
quick_checks.next_step: If wheel roar is constant, stop and schedule cleaning.
quick_checks.risk: Loose blower set screws can throw wheels — $900–$2,800 motor/wheel jobs.

diagnostic_steps.step: Static pressure budget versus design ESP
diagnostic_steps.homeowner: Report which floors cool last after sundown.
diagnostic_steps.pro: Manometer across coil, compare to OEM max external static.
diagnostic_steps.risk: Ignoring static caps voids sensible capacity math.

diagnostic_steps.step: Coil face inspection and wet bulb approach
diagnostic_steps.homeowner: Note musty smell or standing water in pan.
diagnostic_steps.pro: Delta-WB across coil, photograph fin damage, plan pull-clean.
diagnostic_steps.risk: Aggressive fin combing causes leaks — coil change $2,200–$5,500.

diagnostic_steps.step: Duct leakage smoke or pressure pan test
diagnostic_steps.homeowner: List obvious disconnected boots in attic.
diagnostic_steps.pro: Seal major leaks first, then rebalance dampers.
diagnostic_steps.risk: Pulling return from attic without sealing pulls fiberglass into lungs.

repair_matrix.fix: Right-size MERV + media cabinet if needed

cost_escalation.description: Cheap static wins before invasive work.

safe: Swap to manufacturer-allowed lower MERV if suction collapses filter.
Open all registers and dampers for a fair test.
call_pro: Weak airflow persists after new filter and obvious duct fixes.
Grinding blower noise or hot motor smell from cabinet.
stop_now: Electrical burning smell from air handler cabinet.
Visible arcing when blower door is seated.

final_warning: Never run a blower with a frozen coil — you will flood the home and destroy the motor.
cta: If humidity stays high in Tampa despite cool setpoint, book static and CFM verification before any refrigerant work.

title: AC Freezing Up in Tampa, FL — Airflow, Coil, and Charge Logic

summary_30s.headline: Ice on the indoor coil is a symptom, not a diagnosis: you must separate low airflow from low pressure conditions before adding or removing refrigerant.

top_causes.label: Low indoor airflow freezing the coil
top_causes.probability: Highest first check in Florida dust/pollen

top_causes.label: Low refrigerant lowering evap pressure
top_causes.probability: Common when ice forms on line set too

top_causes.label: Metering device or filter-drier restriction
top_causes.probability: Lower but shows uniform ice line

summary_30s.core_truth: Ice insulates the coil from air; the longer you run it frozen, the more liquid can slug back toward the compressor.
summary_30s.risk_warning: Slugged compressors fail mechanically; compressor and coil replacements together often exceed $1,500–$8,500 when homeowners chip ice or keep running frozen.

quick_checks.check: Turn cooling off, fan ON — does ice melt in under 45 minutes?
quick_checks.homeowner: Leave fan only to thaw safely with pan intact.
quick_checks.result_meaning: Fast melt with strong fan suggests airflow return after thaw.
quick_checks.next_step: Replace filter before restart; watch for immediate re-ice.
quick_checks.risk: Chiseling the coil converts a service call into a coil replacement.

quick_checks.check: Liquid line sweating or warm when coil is frozen?
quick_checks.homeowner: Visual only; do not touch lines without gloves.
quick_checks.result_meaning: Warm liquid with frozen coil hints metering or charge issues.
quick_checks.next_step: Photo ice pattern for the tech — top versus bottom matters.
quick_checks.risk: Opening gauges DIY vents refrigerant illegally and skews readings.

quick_checks.check: Drain pan overflow or float tripped?
quick_checks.homeowner: Look for water in secondary pan or stat lockout code.
quick_checks.result_meaning: Ice blocked drain can trip float and mimic dead system.
quick_checks.next_step: Wet vac primary outlet if accessible; stop if black mold smell.
quick_checks.risk: Masking drain issues lets sheetrock flood overnight.

diagnostic_steps.homeowner: Towels on floor, shop vac ready, no cooling until dry.
diagnostic_steps.pro: Verify blower amps and wheel cleanliness post-thaw.
diagnostic_steps.risk: Restarting cool on still-wet coil re-ices in minutes.

diagnostic_steps.homeowner: Confirm all supplies open and damper positions normal.
diagnostic_steps.pro: External static test and design CFM target check.
diagnostic_steps.risk: Charging on a frozen coil always lies to your head pressures.

diagnostic_steps.homeowner: No DIY top-off — EPA and accuracy matter.
diagnostic_steps.pro: Weigh-in, superheat/subcooling, search micro-leaks on indoor coil.
diagnostic_steps.risk: Overcharge after thaw spikes head and blows the cap.

nodes.label: Re-ice test after filter path clear

repair_matrix.issue: Primary drain clog from ice slush

repair_matrix.fix: Repair flare, braze, vacuum, weigh charge

cost_escalation.description: Comfort crash, water risk, compressor stress.
Slugged valve plate or grounded compressor.

safe: Thaw with fan-only and dry towels once power to cool is off.
Replace visibly loaded filter after thaw.
call_pro: Ice returns same day after clean filter and thaw.
Oil stain on line set or audible hiss when frost melts.
stop_now: Grinding compressor noise after ice event.
Breaker trips when blower or cool engages post-thaw.

final_warning: Never use sharp tools or pressure washers on a frozen evaporator coil.
cta: After one controlled thaw cycle, if ice rebuilds, shut cooling and call a Tampa tech for airflow numbers before any refrigerant adjustment.

title: AC Making Noise in Tampa, FL — Classify the Sound Before Replacing Parts

summary_30s.headline: HVAC noise complaints separate into airflow whistles, bearing squeals, refrigerant hiss, and hard mechanical knocks — each path drives different cost and urgency.

top_causes.label: Outdoor fan motor bearing or blade imbalance
Compressor mounting or internal valve noise
top_causes.probability: Medium; often misread as fan issue

top_causes.label: Refrigerant velocity or leak-related hiss
top_causes.probability: Lower but highest urgency when sharp

summary_30s.core_truth: Intermittent clicks at contactor pull-in differ from continuous screech; recording ten seconds of audio saves mis-ordered parts.
summary_30s.risk_warning: Ignoring a repetitive metallic knock on a scroll compressor can end in seized rotor; full outdoor replacements in Tampa frequently run $1,500–$9,000 versus timely $350–$900 fan motor service.

quick_checks.check: Indoor vs outdoor: where is the sound loudest?
quick_checks.homeowner: Stand at supply grille, then at condenser fence.
quick_checks.result_meaning: Indoor whistles point to duct or blower; outdoor screech points to fan or compressor.
quick_checks.next_step: Note if pitch changes when stat fan-only runs.
quick_checks.risk: Opening outdoor grille without training risks fan blade cuts.

quick_checks.check: Fan blade wobble or contact scrape mark on shroud?
quick_checks.homeowner: Power off at disconnect, look through top grate only.
quick_checks.result_meaning: Tip rub means loose blade or failing motor bearing.
quick_checks.next_step: Do not bend blades by hand — balance is measured.
quick_checks.risk: Running a rubbing blade cuts shroud and throws metal into coil.

quick_checks.check: Hiss that changes with indoor coil icing?
quick_checks.homeowner: Look for frost while sound is present.
quick_checks.result_meaning: Velocity noise climbs as coil ices; also consider leak ultrasonic.
quick_checks.next_step: Shut system if hiss is sharp like escaping pressure.
quick_checks.risk: High-side refrigerant spray causes frostbite and blindness.

diagnostic_steps.step: Classify frequency: constant vs once per minute
diagnostic_steps.homeowner: Use phone voice memo at both units.
diagnostic_steps.pro: Stethoscope on compressor dome vs discharge line.
diagnostic_steps.risk: Mislabeling valve flutter as loose screw delays compressor swap.

diagnostic_steps.step: Outdoor fan amp draw and spin-down test
diagnostic_steps.pro: Compare FLA to nameplate; check bearing play vertically.
diagnostic_steps.risk: Seized fan motor overheats windings and trips breaker.

diagnostic_steps.homeowner: Do not load unit with cool if knock is metallic.
diagnostic_steps.pro: Megohm, amp signature, suction/discharge vibration pads.
diagnostic_steps.risk: Continued operation throws rods through shell — catastrophic leak.

cost_escalation.description: Rattle panels, loose screws, minor rub.

safe: Tighten accessible panel screws after power is verified off.
Record audio clip for the dispatcher to route correct tech.
call_pro: Continuous screech or grinding from outdoor unit.
Knock that tracks with compressor ramp speed.
stop_now: Sharp hiss with oil mist visible at line set.
Smoke from outdoor fan motor or winding smell.

final_warning: Never stick tools or hands through the condenser guard with power applied.
cta: If noise is metallic, worsening nightly, or paired with warm supply air, shut the system off and schedule a Tampa HVAC noise diagnostic the same week.
